analysis: Cross-country + cross-measure robustness of the transactional-money / inflation regime result
motivation: Methodology audit showed the US Divisia high-regime flip leans on the 1970s; widen across measure and country.
methodology: Non-overlapping annual obs (overlap-corrected). OECD simple-sum narrow money (MANMM101) vs own next-year CPI, US and Japan, like-for-like.

US_OECD_narrow.reading: OECD narrow money predicts US inflation BETTER in LOW regimes — OPPOSITE of the Divisia-M1 high-regime flip found earlier.

Japan_OECD_narrow.reading: Japan DOES show transactional money mattering more in higher-inflation periods (0.352 vs 0.007 on median split).

critical_finding: The 'transactional aggregate wins in high-inflation regimes' result is NOT robust to the narrow-money measure. Divisia M1 (user-cost weighted) showed the flip; OECD simple-sum narrow money shows the reverse for the US. The two countries also disagree on the same OECD measure.
what_survives: The two circuits are distinct objects (Divisia-M1 vs M2 growth corr 0.68) — robust, measure-independent.
The narrow-money/inflation relationship IS regime-dependent in both countries — real but weaker claim.
Deeper framework point holds: no single aggregate mechanically reads inflation; which one carries signal depends on measure, country, regime.
what_does_NOT_survive: 'Transaction-active aggregate reliably out-predicts broad money in high-inflation regimes' as a universal law. It is measure- and country-specific.
caveat_on_earlier_result: The earlier two_circuit_divergence_VERIFIED.json high-regime flip (Divisia 0.138 vs M2 0.075) is real for THAT measure but should NOT be presented as measure-robust. Flag it accordingly if cited.
honest_verdict: Widening WEAKENED the strong claim (correctly). Kept: circuits are distinct + relationship is regime-conditional. Lost: a stable universal direction. This is the accurate, defensible state.

japan_narrow_vs_broad_horserace.basis: OECD MEI: narrow MANMM101 vs broad MABMM301, own next-yr CPI, non-overlapping annual, n=40 (1981-2020)
japan_narrow_vs_broad_horserace.critical_caveat: Japan post-1980 is a chronic LOW-inflation economy: median 0.6%, max 7.4%, only 1 year >=4%. It cannot test the high-money-growth regime the framework's claim is about, because it lacks that regime. What it shows: in low-inflation conditions the transactional aggregate is NOT the better inflation predictor — broad wins in every regime available.
japan_narrow_vs_broad_horserace.reading: Direct contradiction of a naive 'narrow/transactional is universally the better inflation object' reading. Consistent with regime-dependence (narrow matters only in high-inflation regimes), but Japan can't supply that regime to confirm it.

updated_scorecard.survives: Paper 10's ACTUAL claim: CORRECTED (Divisia/composition) transaction aggregates out-inform SIMPLE-SUM M2 in HIGH-money-growth regimes, on US data, as a lower-bound test. Reproduces.
does_not_generalize_to: Japan (broad wins; ~no high regime to test)
updated_scorecard.bottom_line: The Mt-beats-M2 result is US-and-high-regime-and-corrected-aggregate specific. It is NOT a universal 'transactional is the better inflation measure' law. Paper 10 already states it at exactly this bounded strength and refuses the overclaim; the papers need no edit.
